Int. J. Simulation and Process Modelling, Vol. 17, Nos. 2/3, 2021 
Copyright © 2021 Inderscience Enterprises Ltd. 
Systems of distributed artificial intelligence for 
analysis of oil product transportation processes: 
evidence from Russia 
Elena Serova* and Daniil Shklyaev 
HSE University, 
3 Kantemirovskaya St., 
St. Petersburg, 194100, Russia 
Email: egserova@hse.ru 
Email: dshklyaev@hse.ru 
*Corresponding author 
Abstract: At present, the use of artificial intelligence (AI) methods and tools is an essential 
component of management information systems for a company to succeed in a rapidly changing 
environment. Agent-based modelling systems as systems of distributed AI must be considered 
nowadays as an obligatory stage of decision-making in Russian oil and gas companies, which use 
modern information technologies actively. The paper is focused on the description and 
comparative analysis of system dynamics and agent-based modelling, used for intelligent 
decision support systems development in transport logistics. The main goal of this research is 
evaluation of the multi-agent system’s role for decision-making processes and management 
information systems development and creating the model of logistics processes (the processes of 
oil and oil products transportation, loading, and unloading). The work is based on a 
generalisation of theoretical researches in this area along with international practices and 
domestic experience. 
Keywords: artificial intelligence; distributed artificial intelligence systems; simulation;  
agent-based modelling; transport logistics; the processes of oil and oil products transportation; 
Russia. 
Reference to this paper should be made as follows: Serova, E. and Shklyaev, D. (2021) ‘Systems 
of distributed artificial intelligence for analysis of oil product transportation processes: evidence 
from Russia’, Int. J. Simulation and Process Modelling, Vol. 17, Nos. 2/3, pp.106–115. 
Biographical notes: Elena Serova is an Associate Professor working now in the National 
Research University Higher School of Economics St. Petersburg Branch, Russia. Her role 
combines teaching and research in equal measure. She also is the Academic Supervisor of PhD 
students Doctoral Schools of Economics and Management. Her research interests are related to 
artificial intelligent, intellectual data analysis, business analysis, strategic management, 
marketing, and information management. She has co-authored the books and contributed 
chapters to several books and collections of essays, regular key presenter at national and 
international conferences and workshops. As a research-active academic with several PhD 
students under her supervision, she is focusing on complex information systems modelling, 
marketing information systems, and business models in global environment. 
Daniil Shklyaev is currently a Doctoral student at National Research University Higher School of 
Economics in St. Petersburg [Program ‘Economics and Management of the National Economy 
(Logistics)’]. Research theme: ‘Comparative analysis based on simulation modelling for oil 
products logistic schemes of loading and transportation’. He regularly participates in scientific 
conferences. He works at PJSC Gazprom Neft as the Chief Specialist of the Development 
Section of the Logistics and Transport Department. In 2017, he is the winner in the tournament 
‘League of profesSionals’ of PJSC Gazprom Neft, which was held among the logisticians of 
PJSC Gazprom Neft. In 2019, he won the ‘Best Expert’ nomination as part of the Gazprom Neft 
tournament ‘League of Professionals’. His research interests are transport logistics, development 
of logistics infrastructure, modelling, and simulation. 
This paper is a revised and expanded version of a paper entitled ‘Agent-based modeling for 
decision-making support: case of transport logistics in oil company’ presented at the  
32nd European Modeling & Simulation Symposium (I3M, EMSS 2020), 16–18 September 2020, 
virtual.

Sberbank Technologies, Croc, Kaspersky Lab, Avito, MTS, 
Yota, etc. The main feature of these Russian companies is 
that AI has a significant impact on their revenue and makes 
a significant contribution to their business model. 
According to Serova (2020c), intelligent technologies 
add to the globalisation of business by providing quick 
access to employees, customers, and partners worldwide, as 
well as coordinating global interaction between companies 
at different stages of the value chain. It does not mean that 
intelligent technologies and systems simply increase the 
efficiency of a company’s operations; they can be 
considered as a key intangible asset. AI can be considered 
as a set of technological solutions that are at the stage of 
active development, and it has already been successfully 
used in many industries, economic sectors, and companies. 
AI techniques include data mining, database, ML, pattern 
recognition, and knowledge discovery (Serova, 2020c). 
When decision making on the strategic level of 
management, the use of distributed artificial systems such as 
multi-agent systems (MAS), can be considered now as an 
effective tool for performance improvement. Intelligent 
agents (intellectual agents) – programs that independently 
perform tasks that are specified by the user or by other 
programs, as a rule over extended periods, and are used for 
data collection and analysis. According to Yilmaz (2020), 
the ability to systematically vary and experiment with 
multiple model features is paramount for problems that 
involve uncertainty and representational ambiguity. 
Currently, in the context of the development of the 
paradigm of the digital economy around the world, there has 
been a significant increase in interest in the use of 
intelligent methods and systems of distributed AI, the 
implementation 
of 
appropriate 
information 
and 
communication technologies in organisations which are 
seeking to ensure a stable economic situation in an 
aggravated competitive struggle. When decision making on 
the strategic level of management, the use of distributed 
artificial systems such as MAS, can be considered now as 
an effective tool for performance improvement. Intelligent 
agents (intellectual agents) – programs that independently 
perform tasks that are specified by the user or by other 
programs, as a rule over extended periods, and are used for 
data 
collection 
and 
analysis. 
The 
involvement 
of 
information technologies and procedures, as well as modern 
simulation systems, in the first place, MAS – systems of 
distributed AI, allows you to bring the solution of logistics 
problems to quantitative results, which are especially 
valuable for managers. 
The study of problems that occur in the development of 
the theory and practice of logistics is carried out taking into 
account the application of a systematic approach and 
modern technologies, such as intelligence data analysis and 
simulation. Along with others, such a logistics subsystem as 
transport logistics is especially important. The functionality 
of transport logistics is actively used in various sectors of 
the economy. For example, vertically integrated oil 
companies create complex supply chains. Methods and 
means of transport logistics are used to deliver oil from the 
field to refineries, then for loading, and subsequently for 
transportation and discharge of various oil products to 
numerous consumers in the Russian Federation and abroad. 
The purpose of this work is evaluation of the role such 
distributed AI systems as MAS for decision-making 
processes 
and 
management 
information 
systems 
development and creating the model of logistics processes 
(the processes of oil and oil products transportation, 
loading, and unloading). The paper also focuses on 
comparative analysis of such approaches in simulation as 
agent modelling and system dynamics (SD), as applied to 
logistics 
processes 
(the 
processes 
of 
oil 
products 
transportation, loading, and unloading). To achieve this 
goal, it is necessary to develop principles for constructing a 
model for the process of unloading and loading oil products 
in AnyLogic software. 
Modern research claims that decision-makers operate in 
a complex and rapidly changing environment, which is 
characterised by the fact that: 
It is necessary to make several decisions to achieve the 
goal, each of which is considered in the context of other 
decisions. 
• 
Decisions are not independent; each subsequent 
decision is limited by the consequences of previous 
decisions and in turn imposes restrictions on the 
following decisions. 
• 
The decision-making environment changes on its own, 
as well as a result of decisions. 
• 
Decisions are made in real-time. 
The methodology of the research is based on a system 
approach, 
comparative 
analysis, 
and 
methods 
and 
procedures of modelling. 
2 
Theoretical fundamental and brief literature 
review 
The foundations of decision-making research in a dynamic 
environment were laid by Forrester (1961). Further, 
Pospelov (1998), Sterman (2000), Law and Kelton (2000), 
Wooldridge (2002), Pidd (2004) and Albright et al. (2011) 
made their contribution to this area. 
AI and the technologies that support it, such as big data, 
cloud, internet of things (IoT) sensors, and ML, are 
becoming researchers, collaborators, augmented assistants, 
and even proxies for humans in the customer experience 
(SAP SE, 2018). 
It should be highlighting that predictive analytics and 
intelligent data analysis are the key directions of the use of 
AI systems and technologies for decision-making support in 
modern companies (Serova, 2020a). 
In the late 1970s and early 1980s many studies in the 
field of decision-making have been carried out based on 
experiments in the framework of static systems. However, 
over time, the conclusions of such studies were criticised 
and the need arose for conducting experiments in the field 
of decision-making in dynamic systems (DS). Studies
4:
Systems of distributed artificial intelligence for analysis of oil product transportation processes 
109 
appeared in which subjects had to make decisions in 
experimental systems that included feedback, time-lag 
effects, and nonlinear behaviour. This has become possible 
thanks to the use of computer simulation models. 
Currently, the so-called ‘complexity economy’ is 
gaining ground, which refutes the thesis about the desire to 
maximise utility when deciding by a person as an economic 
agent. The concept of economics as a complex adaptive 
system creates the need for a search and implementation of 
a new methodology that allows modelling processes. The 
behaviour of the system is formed from the relationships of 
many agents, each of which has certain behavioural 
features. 
The 
major 
approaches 
(methods) 
in 
simulation 
modelling are SD, discrete event (DE), and agent-based 
(AB). SD and DE are traditional; AB is relatively new. 
There is also DS, but as a rule, it used to model and design 
‘physical’ systems (Borshchev and Filippov, 2004, 2006; 
Karpov, 2005; Serova, 2012a, 2012b, 2012c; Krichevsky 
and Serova, 2016; Elberg and Tsygankov, 2017). 
According 
to 
Serova 
(2012c), 
MAS 
and 
AB 
programming are the next step in the development of  
object-oriented 
programming 
and 
integrate 
the 
achievements in the field of AI, parallel computing, and 
knowledge-based systems. MAS are a self-organising 
decentralised network of agents (software objects). Each 
agent is autonomous and strives to achieve its own goal. 
Changing the purpose of one agent leads to the fact that 
others begin to adapt their behaviour and change 
connections. 
Agent technologies are offering various types of agents, 
models of their behaviour and characteristics, ranges of 
architectures, and libraries of components. 
MAS as systems of distributed AI have the following 
benefit (Serova, 2009): 
• 
They speed up task fulfilment by parallelism and save 
the volume of data transmitted bypassing high-level 
partial solutions to other agents. 
• 
They are flexible by using agents of various capacities 
to carry out a task dynamically in cooperation. 
• 
They are reliable by-passing functions from agents 
unable to carry out a task to other ones (Serova, 2009). 
Agents should have the following characteristics: 
• 
The agent is identifiable, i.e., possesses a set of 
characteristic characteristics and principles that 
determine his behaviour and decision-making process; 
autonomous and can make decisions taking into 
account interaction with other agents while acting 
independently. 
• 
The agent has a specific goal that affects his behaviour. 
• 
Self-learning agent. 
Every MAS consists of the following components (Serova, 
2012c): 
• 
Set of organisational units with a subset of agents and 
objects. 
• 
Set of tasks. 
• 
Environment, i.e., space where agents and objects exist. 
• 
Set of relations between agents. 
• 
Set of agent actions (e.g., operations on objects) 
(Serova, 2012c). 
Another modelling approach is SD. 
SD is a methodology for studying and modelling 
systems that are characterised by feedback cycles in 
complex mutual causal relationships of their parameters. 
This modelling approach was developed by Forrester in the 
1950s (Forrester, 1961). Processes that occur in the real 
world in SD are characterised by the terms of stocks, flows 
between these stocks, and information that determines the 
value of these flows. SD abstract from individual objects 
and events and involves an ‘aggregate’ approach to 
processes. In SD, modelling is a set of interacting positive 
and negative feedbacks and delays, and the structure of the 
system determines its behaviour. After the model is built, it 
is possible to apply a simulation of numerical indicators, 
which in the form of a graph or table will represent the 
behaviour of the system. SD is a traditional approach, but it 
is successfully used for the decision of contemporary 
business tasks (Krichevsky and Serova, 2016; Saryazdi  
et al., 2020). 
The main advantages of agent modelling compared to 
SD are: 
• 
Modelling the behaviour of each of the agents. 
• 
The possibility of adaptation. 
• 
The presence of a mechanism for the formation of goals 
and a mechanism for interaction (communication and 
cooperation) of agents. 
The main disadvantage of agent modelling compared to SD 
is that it is often difficult to develop a detailed agent model 
that takes into account all the cause and effect relationships 
that affect the agent’s decision. Below a comparative table 
(Table 1) of two approaches to simulation is shown a brief 
but thorough description of the literature on the topic of the 
paper as well as recent research projects and their outcomes.

E. Serova and D. Shklyaev 
 
Currently, there is different software in which it is possible 
to conduct a simulation. One of these programs is the 
AnyLogic. AnyLogic is a tool that covers the main areas of 
modelling: SD, agent modelling, etc. 
The basic concept of AnyLogic is that the model is 
described by a set of interacting and simultaneously 
functioning activities, where the active object has its 
functioning and interaction with the environment. 
AnyLogic graphical modelling environment is capable 
of supporting the following processes: 
• 
Design. 
• 
Development. 
• 
Documentation of the model. 
• 
Performance of computer experiments with the model. 
3 
Modelling of transportation, loading and 
unloading oil and oil product 
There are various approaches to designing MAS. Yet, three 
levels can be found: conceptual description, initial design, 
and detailed design. At the first level, one should describe 
the organisational structure, goals, business processes, and 
information support underlying the next level’s ontology. 
The next two levels are where all the elements described 
forming the organisational visualisation, i.e., the virtual 
world where agents act, using the ontology to achieve the 
goals and carry out the tasks set. 
Because the behaviour of each of the participants in the 
processes of transportation, loading, and unloading 
operations with petroleum products is unique, agent 
modelling in AnyLogic was chosen to build the model and 
conduct a series of experiments. 
There are several main stages to create a model  
(Table 2). 
The science and business know examples of AnyLogic 
simulation models for the oil industry: for example, a 
system of sea oil transportation for Novoportovskoye field 
(PJSC Gazprom Neft). Oil from this field is transported via 
a 100-kilometre pipeline to the sea terminal located at Cape 
Kamenny (Guld of Ob), from where oil is transported to 
consumers on Arctic tankers. The main problem is difficult 
to ice conditions, which complicate navigation since most of 
the time the movement of ships in the Gulf of Ob takes 
place along a 500-kilometre canal through ice more than 
two meters thick. In the open part of the Kara Sea, drifting 
ice in some months even completely covers the seawater 
area. So, it was necessary to solve the following tasks: 
• 
To develop a permanent oil export route: at this stage, it 
was required to determine the required number of 
vessels, as well as to understand whether the 
involvement of icebreakers would be required; it was 
also necessary to calculate the cost of fuel for tankers 
and the cost of freighting icebreakers. 
• 
To design a temporary route for oil export: it was 
necessary to determine the throughput of the system 
during the period of use of light tankers with their 
subsequent replacement by tankers of larger capacity. 
• 
To determine the required onshore oil terminal 
capacity: it was necessary to calculate the minimum 
onshore oil terminal volume, sufficient for the entire 
development period of the field.

To solve the above problems, the Krylov State Scientific 
Centre has developed a solution. As part of this solution, the 
modules of the GIS environment, shipbuilding calculations, 
and the AnyLogic logistics simulation model were 
combined, which made it possible to take into account the 
logic of transport operations, the characteristics of ships, 
and environmental conditions in the calculations. 
The vessels in the system were presented as agents. At 
the same time, the model took into account the principles of 
interaction between tankers and icebreakers and the logic of 
choosing the speed of the vessel, depending on the fullness 
of the terminal, the severity of ice conditions, the position of 
other vessels, air temperature and other factors.

5 
Conclusions 
The external environment in which economic systems 
operate and develop cannot be described with analytical 
expressions, and in such a situation, AI technologies, 
systems of distributed AI are the primary means of solving 
various economics and management tasks. Based on the 
multi-agent approach, an effective intelligent data analysis 
tool can be created. It is open and flexibly adaptable to the 
problem being solved, as well as can deeply be integrated 
with other systems. 
MAS as systems of distributed AI are the concept that 
starts an era of network organisations with intellectual 
robots’ collective interaction by offering to switch from 
powerful centralised systems to fully decentralised ones, 
with 
hierarchical 
structure 
replaced 
with 
network 
organisation, rigid bureaucratic ‘from top to bottom’ 
management (based on bosses’ commands for subordinates) 
with negotiations, and planning with flexible agreements. 
As 
a 
result, 
production 
volumes, 
profitability, 
competitiveness, 
and 
mobility 
are 
growing. 
Major 
advantages of the multi-agent approach relate to the 
economic mechanisms of self-organisation and evolution 
that become powerful efficiency drivers and ensure 
enterprise’s stable development and prosperity. 
The application of AB modelling finds its niche in the 
field of solving problems of logistics and supply chain 
management. Thus, at present, for modelling processes of 
oil and oil products transportation, loading, and unloading, 
such types of simulation as AB modelling and SD can be 
used. Agent modelling involves the development of a 
behaviour model for each of the agents. SD, on the other 
hand, abstracts from individual objects and events and 
suggests an ‘aggregate’ approach to processes. As an 
example of the software used for logistics tasks simulation, 
AnyLogic is presented in this paper.
